past_medical_history: Splenectomy at age 38 following motor vehicle accident with splenic rupture
Hypertension, diagnosed 8 years ago, well-controlled on medication
Type 2 diabetes mellitus, diagnosed 5 years ago, managed with oral agents
Gout, intermittent flares approximately twice yearly
Pneumococcal vaccination status: received Pneumovax 23 once approximately 12 years ago, no booster, never received PCV13 or PCV20

medications.indication: Started 2 days ago by PCP for presumed sinusitis/URI

family_history: Father died of myocardial infarction at age 62
Mother alive at 82 with hypertension and osteoarthritis

social_history.smoking: Former smoker, quit 10 years ago, 15 pack-year history
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife in a single-story home
social_history.exercise: Walks 30 minutes daily, occasional weekend hiking

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert but confused. Oriented to person and place, but not to date — states it is 'sometime in January' and cannot specify the year. Intermittently drowsy, requiring repeated verbal stimulation to maintain engagement. Follows simple one-step commands but has difficulty with two-step commands. Speech is coherent but slow, with occasional word-finding pauses. Glasgow Coma Scale score: E3V4M6 = 13.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: Pupils 3 mm bilaterally and reactive to light. Visual fields grossly intact to confrontation. No gaze palsy or nystagmus. Mild photophobia noted — patient grimaces and turns away when penlight is directed at eyes. Facial sensation intact to light touch in all three divisions bilaterally. Face symmetric at rest and with activation. Hearing grossly intact. Palate elevates symmetrically. Tongue midline without atrophy or fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Strength 5/5 in all extremities to confrontation. No pronator drift. No fasciculations. Tone normal throughout.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact in all dermatomes. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally. No sensory extinction on double simultaneous stimulation.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally. Triceps 2+ bilaterally. Brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 2+ bilaterally. Achilles 2+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor bilaterally.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally but performed slowly due to patient's overall lethargy. Heel-to-shin intact bilaterally. No dysmetria or dysdiadochokinesia.
neurological_exam.gait: Not assessed due to patient's altered mental status and clinical instability.
neurological_exam.additional: Nuchal rigidity present — passive neck flexion produces significant resistance and discomfort. Kernig sign positive bilaterally. Brudzinski sign equivocal. No papilledema on fundoscopic examination. No focal neurological deficits. No rash or petechiae on skin examination.

patient.chief_complaint: Headache, confusion, and low-grade fever worsening over the past 24 hours
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Kenji Watanabe is a 68-year-old right-handed man with a history of splenectomy, hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and gout who presents to the emergency department with progressive headache, confusion, and fever over approximately 24 hours. According to his wife, who accompanies him, the patient began complaining of a dull, bifrontal headache two days ago, which he attributed to a lingering upper respiratory infection he had been experiencing for about a week. He was seen by his primary care physician the day prior who diagnosed him with likely sinusitis and started him on amoxicillin 500 mg three times daily. Over the past 24 hours, the headache has markedly intensified, becoming constant and rated 8 out of 10 in severity. His wife reports that he became progressively more confused throughout the day, initially repeating himself and seeming 'foggy,' then becoming difficult to arouse from sleep this evening. She also noted that he felt warm to touch, though his temperature at home was measured at only 37.9 degrees Celsius earlier in the day. He has complained of neck stiffness and has vomited twice in the past six hours. He has had no seizure activity, no weakness, no visual changes, and no rash. His wife specifically notes that his confusion is quite unlike him, as he is normally sharp and articulate. She decided to bring him to the emergency department when he was unable to recall their home address and appeared increasingly somnolent. Of particular concern is his history of splenectomy, which places him at significantly elevated risk for infections with encapsulated organisms such as Streptococcus pneumoniae, Neisseria meningitidis, and Haemophilus influenzae. His pneumococcal vaccination status is suboptimal, having received only a single dose of Pneumovax 23 approximately 12 years ago without any subsequent boosters or conjugate vaccines. The partially treated course with oral amoxicillin raises concern that initial cerebrospinal fluid studies may be partially modified, potentially complicating interpretation.

findings.location: Bilateral cerebral convexities, more prominent along the Sylvian fissures

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Subtle linear enhancement along the leptomeninges
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Mild sulcal FLAIR hyperintensity in bilateral frontoparietal regions
signal_characteristics.DWI: No restricted diffusion in the brain parenchyma

additional_observations: No subdural empyema or epidural abscess identified
Paranasal sinuses show mild mucosal thickening in the maxillary sinuses bilaterally, compatible with sinusitis
mri.impression: Incidental bilateral maxillary sinusitis. No intracranial complications such as empyema, abscess, or infarction. Clinical correlation recommended.

labs.interpretation: Abnormal values: WBC 18.4 x10^3/uL (H), Neutrophils 83 % (H), Bands 13 % (H), Lymphocytes 7 % (L), Sodium 122 mEq/L (L), Chloride 91 mEq/L (L), Bicarbonate 18 mEq/L (L), BUN 23 mg/dL (H), Glucose 167 mg/dL (H), CRP 195 mg/L (H), Procalcitonin 9.2 ng/mL (H), ESR 66 mm/hr (H), PT 13.6 seconds (H), INR 1.3  (H), D-dimer 3.0 ug/mL FEU (H), Lactate 3.4 mmol/L (H).

special_tests.gram_stain: No organisms seen (possibly due to prior antibiotic therapy)
special_tests.csf_culture: Pending — preliminary no growth at 24 hours

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 29 cmH2O. WBC: 348 cells/uL (elevated) (72% PMN/25% lymph). Protein: 152.5 mg/dL (elevated; reference: 15-45 mg/dL). Glucose: 34.9 mg/dL (low; reference: 40-70 mg/dL) (ratio 0.19 (low; serum glucose 168 mg/dL; reference: >0.6)). gram_stain: No organisms seen (possibly due to prior antibiotic therapy). latex_agglutination: Positive for Streptococcus pneumoniae antigen. csf_lactate: 6.8 mmol/L (elevated; reference: <3.5 mmol/L).

output.interpretation: Opening pressure: 17 cmH2O. WBC: 85 cells/uL (decreased from 380) (30% PMN/62% lymph). Protein: 93.3 mg/dL (decreased from 165 mg/dL; reference: 15-45 mg/dL). Glucose: 44.0 mg/dL (improved; reference: 40-70 mg/dL) (ratio 0.38 (improved from 0.19; serum glucose 126 mg/dL)). gram_stain: No organisms seen. csf_culture: No growth at 48 hours. csf_lactate: 3.8 mmol/L (decreased from 6.8 mmol/L).

output.impression: Non-contrast CT head is unremarkable. No contraindication to lumbar puncture. Incidental bilateral maxillary sinusitis. Clinical correlation recommended.

Blood cultures.value: Penicillin MIC 0.12 ug/mL (intermediate for meningitis); Ceftriaxone MIC <0.5 ug/mL (susceptible); Vancomycin MIC 0.5 ug/mL (susceptible); Meropenem MIC <0.25 ug/mL (susceptible)

findings.value: Type A bilaterally (normal middle ear function)

quantitative_data.Tympanometry: Type A bilaterally (normal middle ear function)

output.impression: Abnormal values: Right ear pure-tone average (500, 1000, 2000, 4000 Hz) 35 dB HL dB HL (H), Right ear speech recognition threshold 35 dB HL dB HL (H), Right ear word recognition score 88% % (H).

interactions: Ceftriaxone + Metformin: No significant interaction. Monitor renal function.
contraindications: Do not co-administer with calcium-containing IV solutions in neonates (not applicable to this patient)
warnings: Patient reports penicillin allergy (childhood rash) — cross-reactivity with cephalosporins is low (1-2%) for non-anaphylactic reactions. Amoxicillin tolerance suggests safe to administer ceftriaxone.
Monitor for Clostridioides difficile-associated diarrhea with prolonged use
alternatives: Meropenem 2g IV every 8 hours if cephalosporin allergy confirmed
Chloramphenicol 1g IV every 6 hours as alternative in severe allergy

output.proposed: Dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV every 6 hours for 4 days
interactions: Dexamethasone + Metformin: Corticosteroids may increase blood glucose, potentially reducing metformin efficacy. Monitor blood glucose closely.
Dexamethasone + Lisinopril: Corticosteroids may cause sodium retention and antagonize antihypertensive effect. Monitor blood pressure.
warnings: Dexamethasone should ideally be administered before or concurrent with first dose of antibiotics for maximal benefit in bacterial meningitis (IDSA guideline)
May mask fever as a clinical indicator of treatment response
Monitor blood glucose frequently given patient's diabetes — anticipate need for insulin
May reduce CSF penetration of vancomycin — important consideration if Streptococcus pneumoniae with elevated ceftriaxone MIC

differential.key_features: Would expect lymphocytic predominance, normal glucose, lower protein, less systemic toxicity. Partially treated bacterial meningitis can mimic viral pattern but CSF glucose ratio and procalcitonin argue against.
Typically subacute over weeks, basilar meningeal enhancement, very low glucose, lymphocytic predominance. No TB risk factors in this patient.
Typically in immunocompromised (HIV/AIDS), subacute presentation, positive India ink or cryptococcal antigen. Patient is not severely immunocompromised despite asplenia.
Would expect temporal lobe involvement on MRI, seizures, more prominent personality/behavioral changes. No temporal lobe findings on MRI.
Sudden thunderclap headache, xanthochromia and RBCs in CSF. Gradual onset over 24 hours and CSF profile do not support.

optimal_actions.action: Assess airway, breathing, circulation. Obtain IV access and begin fluid resuscitation with normal saline given signs of sepsis (tachycardia, elevated lactate).
Obtain blood cultures (2 sets from separate venipuncture sites) BEFORE administering antibiotics.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Blood cultures will eventually grow Streptococcus pneumoniae

optimal_actions.action: Administer dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg IV immediately — must be given before or with first antibiotic dose for maximal benefit in suspected bacterial meningitis.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduces mortality and hearing loss in pneumococcal meningitis

optimal_actions.action: Initiate empiric IV antibiotics immediately: ceftriaxone 2g IV plus vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV. Do NOT delay for imaging or LP.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Broad coverage for common bacterial meningitis pathogens including resistant pneumococcus

optimal_actions.action: Order CT head without contrast to rule out mass lesion or elevated ICP before lumbar puncture, given altered mental status.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: No mass lesion, no midline shift, safe to proceed with LP

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with opening pressure measurement. Send CSF for cell count with differential, protein, glucose, Gram stain, culture, and latex agglutination/PCR panel.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Elevated opening pressure, mixed pleocytosis with neutrophil predominance, elevated protein, low glucose, low glucose ratio

optimal_actions.action: Order comprehensive labs: CBC with differential, BMP, CRP, procalcitonin, coagulation studies, lactate, blood glucose (for CSF/serum glucose ratio).
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Leukocytosis with left shift, elevated inflammatory markers, hyponatremia, elevated lactate

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain with gadolinium contrast to evaluate for meningeal enhancement and complications (empyema, cerebritis, infarction).
Order CSF meningitis/encephalitis multiplex PCR panel (FilmArray) for rapid pathogen identification, especially given negative Gram stain from partially treated infection.
Admit to ICU for close neurological monitoring, hemodynamic management, and continued IV antibiotics. Implement seizure precautions.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Appropriate level of care for bacterial meningitis with altered mental status

optimal_actions.action: Monitor blood glucose closely and initiate insulin sliding scale or drip given dexamethasone use in a diabetic patient.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Glycemic control maintained despite corticosteroid therapy

optimal_actions.action: Once culture susceptibilities are available, de-escalate antibiotic therapy as appropriate. Consider stopping vancomycin if pneumococcus is ceftriaxone-susceptible.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Narrowed antibiotic coverage based on susceptibilities

optimal_actions.action: Order repeat lumbar puncture at 48 hours if clinical improvement is not evident, to assess treatment response.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Decreasing WBC count, improving glucose, decreasing protein

optimal_actions.action: Arrange audiometry before discharge to screen for sensorineural hearing loss, a common complication of pneumococcal meningitis.
Plan for vaccination update upon recovery: PCV20 followed by PPSV23 at least 8 weeks later, given asplenic status and inadequate prior vaccination.
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Improved protection against future encapsulated organism infections

critical_actions: Administer empiric IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone + vancomycin) immediately — do NOT delay for LP or imaging
Administer dexamethasone before or with first dose of antibiotics
Perform lumbar puncture (after CT if altered mental status) and send CSF for complete analysis
Recognize asplenic status as major risk factor for encapsulated organism infection
Admit to ICU for close monitoring given altered mental status
Monitor for and manage complications: seizures, cerebral edema, SIADH, DIC, hearing loss
contraindicated_actions: Delaying antibiotics to wait for CT scan or lumbar puncture results
Performing lumbar puncture without prior CT imaging in a patient with altered mental status (risk of herniation if mass lesion present)
Administering dexamethasone after antibiotics have already been given for several hours (diminished benefit)
Using oral antibiotics alone for suspected bacterial meningitis
Discharging patient home from the emergency department
Omitting vancomycin from the empiric regimen (risk of penicillin/cephalosporin-resistant pneumococcus)
Ignoring hyponatremia without monitoring — risk of cerebral edema from SIADH
key_reasoning_points: Post-splenectomy patients are at significantly increased risk for overwhelming infections with encapsulated organisms (S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis, H. influenzae) — this should raise clinical suspicion for bacterial meningitis even with a partially treated or atypical presentation.

Prior oral antibiotic therapy (amoxicillin) can partially treat bacterial meningitis, resulting in modified CSF findings: lower WBC count, mixed rather than purely neutrophilic pleocytosis, negative Gram stain, and negative cultures. This makes the diagnosis more challenging and increases reliance on antigen testing (latex agglutination) and PCR.
The low-grade fever (38.3°C) rather than high fever is characteristic of partially treated meningitis and should not be falsely reassuring.

Dexamethasone has proven mortality and morbidity benefit specifically in pneumococcal meningitis when given before or with the first antibiotic dose (de Gans trial). It reduces hearing loss, which is a major complication.
The CSF-to-serum glucose ratio (0.19) is more diagnostically useful than the absolute CSF glucose alone, especially in a diabetic patient with elevated serum glucose.
Hyponatremia (Na 131) in the setting of CNS infection suggests SIADH, which requires monitoring and possible fluid restriction to prevent worsening cerebral edema.
Suboptimal pneumococcal vaccination in an asplenic patient represents a preventable risk factor — vaccination counseling is an important part of follow-up care.

clinical_pearls: In partially treated bacterial meningitis, Gram stain sensitivity drops from ~60-90% to <50%, making PCR and antigen testing critical for pathogen identification.
Asplenic patients should receive empiric coverage for encapsulated organisms until cultures definitively rule them out.
Dexamethasone benefit in bacterial meningitis is most strongly supported for pneumococcal disease and diminishes if given after antibiotics have been started.
